metadata.summary: Deterministic percentage rollout via MD5 hash bucketing, plus explicit user-list and group-based targeting. Enables gradual 1%→10%→50%→100% progressions with consistent per-user assignment.

content.title: Feature Flag Rollout and Targeting
content.summary: Gradual rollouts require deterministic per-user assignment: the same user must consistently see the same variant. The canonical approach combines MD5 hash bucketing for percentage rollout with an explicit allowlist (user IDs) and group membership (beta_testers, employees) evaluated in priority order. Couple rollout percentage changes to error-rate alerts to enable automatic rollback.

why.p: Non-deterministic rollout (random per-request) causes the same user to see the new feature on one page load and the old feature on the next — a broken experience that makes A/B analytics meaningless and support tickets hard to reproduce. Hash bucketing on (flag_name + user_id) ensures consistent assignment with uniform distribution across the user population.

The priority order (explicit list → group membership → percentage) allows teams to pre-enroll internal users and beta testers before the percentage reaches them, without overriding the percentage logic for everyone else.

list.item: Gradual feature rollouts where you want 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% progression with monitoring at each step.
Beta programs where specific employees or opted-in users always see the new feature before general availability.
A/B experiments where each user must consistently see the same variant for the experiment to be statistically valid.
Group-based feature access: beta_testers, employees, premium_tier — evaluated before the percentage bucket.
Anonymous traffic where no stable user_id exists — you cannot maintain consistent assignment without a persistent identifier.
Features that must be either fully on or fully off for all users simultaneously — use a simple boolean flag instead.
High-volume paths where hash computation is too expensive — pre-evaluate at session start and cache in the request context.

section.name: percentage-rollout
section.p: The RolloutConfig holds a percentage (0–100), an explicit user_ids set (always enabled), and a groups set (enabled for members). FeatureFlagManagerWithRollout evaluates these in priority order before falling back to the percentage hash.

example.title: Deterministic percentage rollout with user and group targeting
example.note: The key f"{flag_name}:{user_id}" ensures that a user in bucket 15 for "new_search" may be in bucket 72 for "new_checkout" — independent assignment per flag.

section.name: rollout-progression
section.p: A safe rollout progression couples each percentage increase to an error-rate check. Automate the progression or require a human sign-off at each step.

how-to.step: Start at 0%: internal QA only, using the explicit user_ids allowlist.
1%: deploy to a small slice; establish baseline error rate and latency for new vs old path.
10%: monitor for 24 hours; if p99 latency or error rate is within 5% of baseline, proceed.
50%: monitor for 48 hours; check product metrics (conversion, engagement) in addition to technical metrics.
100%: enable for all; schedule cleanup task within the flag's expected lifespan.

rule.statement: Couple each rollout percentage increase to an automated error-rate check. If the new code path shows an error-rate spike above 0.5% over the baseline, automatically revert the percentage to 0 and page the owner.
rule.rationale: Manual monitoring is unreliable at 2am. An automated rollback gate catches regressions within minutes and prevents a 1% experiment from silently degrading 1% of production traffic for days.

section.name: launchdarkly-targeting
section.p: When using a managed flag service such as LaunchDarkly, the same priority order applies but targeting rules are configured in the UI. The Python SDK call builds a Context object with user attributes and evaluates the remote targeting rules.

example.title: LaunchDarkly evaluation with user context
example.note: Always pass a safe default to ld_client.variation(). If LaunchDarkly is unavailable, the default is returned — ensure the default keeps the old (proven) behavior active.

antipattern.description: A/B leakage: a user opens two tabs and sees two variants because the assignment is session-scoped, not user-scoped.
antipattern.reason: Session-scoped assignment makes analytics treat one user as two. Always key on a stable user identifier (user_id), not session token or cookie.

antipattern.description: Global flags without user context: flag applies to all traffic or none.
antipattern.reason: Without user_id, gradual rollout is impossible. The flag becomes a deploy-time toggle, not a progressive delivery tool.

antipattern.description: LaunchDarkly (or any SaaS flag service) outage causes your app outage because is_enabled() blocks on the SDK call.
antipattern.reason: Always configure a local in-memory cache on the SDK with a stale-while-revalidate policy. The cached evaluation runs even when the flag service is unreachable.
